summary: A Turkic-Islamic empire founded around 1299 by Osman I in Bithynia (northwestern Anatolia) that grew from a small beylik into a transcontinental power ruled by the House of Osman until 1922-1923. Its sultans, based first at Bursa and Edirne and then, after Mehmed II's 1453 conquest, at Constantinople, presided over institutions like the devshirme child-levy that fed the Janissary corps and the millet system that governed religious minorities. The empire reached its height under Suleiman the Magnificent, clashed repeatedly with the rival Safavid Empire (Battle of Chaldiran) and with Christian Europe (Battles of Mohacs, Preveza, Lepanto, sieges of Vienna and Malta), and was later known as the "sick man of Europe" as it lost Balkan territory to nationalist uprisings and Russo-Ottoman wars. Its final collapse followed the Young Turk Revolution, the Arab Revolt and Gallipoli campaign of World War I, and postwar partition (Treaty of Sevres, Sykes-Picot), ending with Mustafa Kemal Ataturk's abolition of the sultanate and, in 1924, the caliphate.

works.name: General / Giveaway Identifiers
works.description: How the empire is identified in giveaways: a religiously diverse, Turkic-Islamic empire ruled by sultans of the House of Osman from Constantinople from 1453 to 1923 (founded c. 1299), whose rulers included Osman I, Murad, Mehmed, and Suleiman the Magnificent. It is closely tied in cluesets to its founder's dream-legend, its devshirme/millet systems, and its eventual successor state, modern Turkey.

clues.clue: A religiously diverse empire ruled by sultans of the House of Osman from Constantinople, lasting from 1453 (or its founding c. 1299) to 1923
examples: name this religiously-diverse empire ruled by sultans in Constantinople from 1453 to 1923.
name this empire founded by Osman, which saw Sultans such as Murad and Mehmed rule from Constantinople.

clues.clue: Founded by Osman I; later ruled by Suleyman/Suleiman the Magnificent
examples: name this empire ruled by Osman I and Suleyman the Magnificent.
name this empire ruled by sultans like Osman I.

clues.clue: Founding legend: Osman I dreamed of a moon entering his chest and a tree sprouting from his navel/chest and shading the whole world, before winning the Battle of Bapheus
examples: In a mythical story, the founder of this empire had a dream of a tree sprouting from his navel and shading the world; that ruler's grandson founded the devshirme system.
One ruler of this empire saw fountains and gardens sheltered by a tree growing from a man's navel in a dream before founding this empire, and that man won the Battle of Bapheus.

clues.clue: Its crescent-and-star symbol may have been adopted from the earlier Byzantine Empire; the founding dream may also explain the crescent's presence on its flag
examples: This empire, which practiced devshirme in the Balkans, possibly adopted the crescent symbol from the earlier Byzantines.
His dream may have inspired the crescent and star found on the flag of this empire.

clues.clue: Contemporary Turkish nationalists have invoked its expansionary "red apple" myth to promote irredentism
examples: Contemporary nationalists have used this polity's expansionary "red apple" myth to promote irredentism.

works.name: Founding and the Ghaza Thesis Debate (13th-14th century)
works.description: The empire began as one of several Anatolian beyliks that emerged after the Mongols shattered the Seljuk Sultanate of Rum at the Battle of Kose Dag. Osman I, chief of the Kayı tribe of Oghuz Turks, subjugated neighboring beyliks from a base in Bithynia and won the empire's first recorded military victory at the Battle of Bapheus; his son Orhan captured Nicomedia and, advised by his half-brother Alaeddin, organized an early military system. Historians have long debated the motor behind this early growth: Paul Wittek's now-discredited "Ghaza thesis" attributed it to holy war against Byzantium, a view Rudi Paul Lindner countered by emphasizing tribal organization and the assimilation of diverse populations, and which Colin Imber attacked by calling the early period a documentary "black hole."

clues.clue: Osman I led the Kayı tribe of Oghuz Turks, subjugating surrounding beyliks that emerged after Mongol influence waned and the Sultanate of Rum fragmented into small principalities
examples: After Mongol influence waned, Osman Ghazi led his Kayı tribe to subjugate surrounding beyliks into this gunpowder empire that besieged Constantinople in 1453.
identify this empire that started as a small state in western Anatolia, founded by Osman I.

clues.clue: The mythologized founder settled his people (of the Oghuz tribe) in the region of Bithynia; the empire's first military victory came at the Battle of Bapheus
examples: This empire's mythologized founder was a leader of the Oghuz tribe who settled his people in Bithynia.
The first of few historical records of the founder of this empire was his victory at the Battle of Bapheus.

clues.clue: During the roughly forty-year rule of its second ruler (Orhan), he was advised by his half-brother Aleaddin on a new military system after capturing the city of Nicomedia
examples: During the almost forty year rule of the second ruler of this empire, was advised by his half-brother Aleaddin on a new military system after he captured the city of Nicomedia.

clues.clue: Paul Wittek's now-discredited "Ghaza thesis" attributed the empire's early growth to holy war; the theory also held it was founded by tribes fleeing the Mongols
examples: Though now discredited, Paul Wittek posited that this empire's early growth was fueled by holy war in the ghaza thesis.
The now-disputed Ghaza thesis states that this empire was founded by tribes fleeing the Mongols.

clues.clue: Historian Rudi Paul Lindner instead proposed the empire's rise came from tribal organization and assimilation of diverse populations, opposing Wittek's "holy war" theory; Colin Imber separately called the early period a documentary "black hole"
examples: Historian Rudi Paul Litner proposed that the rise of this empire came from tribal organization and the assimilation of diverse populations, in opposition to the popular 'holy war' theory.
Colin Imber attacked a theory about this empire's early history by calling the period a 'black hole.'

clues.clue: Members of the Candarlı family repeatedly served as advisors to its early rulers
examples: Several members of the Candarlı family advised rulers of this empire.

works.name: Conquest of Constantinople and Mehmed II
works.description: The empire's defining early triumph was Mehmed II's 1453 capture of Constantinople, ending the Byzantine Empire and the Palaiologos dynasty. After negotiating with the Hungarian engineer Orban, the Ottomans deployed a giant siege cannon nicknamed the "Basilic" and moved their capital from Edirne to the newly conquered city. Mehmed II appointed the Christian Patriarch Gennadius II to a position of power within the empire as part of governing his religious minorities, and was later portrayed by Gentile Bellini in a famous 1480 portrait.

clues.clue: After negotiations with the Hungarian engineer Orban, the empire acquired a giant siege cannon nicknamed the "Basilic," used to help conquer the city it made its new capital in 1453 (moving from Edirne)
examples: after negotiations with Orban of Hungary, this empire bought a large piece of siege artillery, nicknamed the 'Basilic'.
This empire moved its capital from Edirne to a city which it conquered with the help of the 'Basilic' cannon in 1453.

clues.clue: The 1453 siege of Constantinople ended the Byzantine Empire, supposedly turning its last emperor to marble as the Palaiologos dynasty fell
examples: An emperor supposedly turned to marble after a siege between these two empires that caused the fall of the Paleologos dynasty.
what two empires fought each other during the 1453 siege of Constantinople?

clues.clue: Mehmed II appointed the Christian Patriarch Gennadius II to a position of power within the empire, an early example of its governance of Christian minorities
examples: Patriarch Gennadius II was the first Christian leader to be appointed to a position of power within this polity.

clues.clue: Mehmed II is the subject of a famous 1480 portrait by the Venetian painter Gentile Bellini
examples: name these rulers, one of whom is the subject of Gentile Bellini's Portrait of Mehmed II.

clues.clue: Mehmed II ('the Conqueror') captured Constantinople and later dispersed the people of the Karamanid Dynasty and defeated the Qizilbash of Ismail I at Chaldiran (conflating two rulers in some cluesets)
examples: This empire scattered the people of to the Karamanid Dynasty, and defeated the Qızılbash of Ismail I at the Battle of Chaldiran.

works.name: Suleiman the Magnificent, the Franco-Ottoman Alliance, and Mediterranean/Indian Ocean Naval Power
works.description: Under Suleiman the Magnificent -- styled "the Lawgiver" domestically -- the empire reached its territorial and cultural zenith. Suleiman controversially allied with Francis I of France against their common Habsburg rivals, jointly besieging Nice, and fought the Habsburgs at the Battle of Mohacs and in repeated sieges of Vienna. His admiral Hayreddin Barbarossa defeated Andrea Doria at the 1538 Battle of Preveza, cementing Mediterranean naval supremacy that lasted until the 1571 Battle of Lepanto, though a 1565 siege of Malta was repelled by Jean de La Valette's Knights Hospitaller. Beyond the Mediterranean, the admiral and cartographer Piri Reis fought Portugal for control of the Indian Ocean, recapturing Aden but failing to retake Hormuz, and compiled the Kitab-i Bahriye and a celebrated 1513 world map.

clues.clue: Suleiman the Magnificent controversially allied with Francis I of France against the Habsburgs, jointly besieging Nice in 1543; the two sides had earlier been drawn together after Francis's capture at the 1525 Battle of Pavia/Battle of Madrid
examples: name these two powers who controversially allied under Suleiman the Magnificent and Francis I against the Habsburgs.
Francis I and this empire joined forces to capture the port city of Nice.

clues.clue: The empire's ruler was dubbed "the Lawgiver" domestically while Westerners called him "the Magnificent"
examples: This empire dubbed its most powerful leader as 'the lawgiver,' while westerners referred to him as 'the magnificent.'

clues.clue: The captured French king's imprisonment after the Battle of Pavia was a factor in Ottoman invasion of Hungary, leading to the Battle of Mohacs
examples: The imprisonment of a monarch of one of these powers after the Battle of Pavia was a factor in the invasion of Hungary by the other, leading to the Battle of Mohacs.

clues.clue: Admiral Hayreddin Barbarossa defeated Andrea Doria at the 1538 Battle of Preveza, securing Mediterranean naval supremacy until defeat by Holy League forces at Lepanto (1571); a 1565 siege of Malta was repelled by Jean de La Valette's Knights Hospitaller
examples: Andrea Doria was defeated by this empire's admiral Hayreddin Barbarossa at the 1538 Battle of Preveza, which solidified this empire's naval supremacy until it was defeated by Holy League forces.
Jean de La Valette led the Knights Hospitaller in a successful defense against this empire's siege of Malta.

clues.clue: The empire's Mediterranean naval dominance ended at the 1571 Battle of Lepanto
examples: name this empire whose domination of the Mediterranean was ended at the Battle of Lepanto.

clues.clue: The admiral and cartographer Piri Reis fought Portugal for control of the Indian Ocean, recapturing Aden but failing to retake Hormuz in 1552, and compiled the Kitab-i Bahriye (Book of Navigation) and a 1513 world map on gazelle parchment
examples: Piri Reis recaptured Aden but failed to retake Hormuz from one of these countries in 1552.
Piri Reis was an admiral in the service of this empire, for which he compiled the Kitab-i Bahriye, or Book of Navigation.

clues.clue: The empire and Portugal vied for hegemony over the Indian Ocean; Francisco de Almeida's victory at the Battle of Diu over an Ottoman-Mamluk-Gujarati coalition was a major setback, and the empire later aided the Sultanate of Aceh against Portuguese-held Malacca
examples: Francisco de Almeida emerged victorious in a clash between these two nations, winning the Battle of Diu against the other's navy, their Mamluk vassals, the Sultan of Gujarat, and the Zamorin of Calicut.
One of these non-Dutch countries taught the art of cannon-making to the Sultanate of Aceh and supported it during its siege of Malacca, which was held by the other from 1511 to 1641.

works.name: Rivalry with the Safavid Empire
works.description: As a Sunni power, the empire repeatedly fought the Shia Safavid Empire of Iran. Selim I ("the Grim") crushed Shah Ismail I's Qizilbash forces at the decisive 1514 Battle of Chaldiran, after which he turned south to conquer the Mamluk Sultanate (taking Mecca and, with it, the title of caliph). Later fighting included the week-long "Battle of the Torches," and the rivalry was periodically formalized by treaties such as the Peace of Amasya and its later consolidation, the Treaty of Zuhab.

clues.clue: Selim I ("the Grim") crushed Shah Ismail I's Qizilbash forces at the 1514 Battle of Chaldiran, a defeat that shattered Ismail
examples: That army of one of these powers was composed of the Qizilbash, but was defeated by the other side at the Battle of Chaldiran by forces led by Selim the Grim, a loss that shattered Ismail I.
name these two empires whose greatest leaders were Suleiman the Magnificent and Shah Abbas I.

clues.clue: After Chaldiran, Selim I turned south and conquered the Mamluk Sultanate, seizing Mecca and Egypt in 1517
examples: After the Battle of Chaldiran, at which this empire repulsed Safavid incursions, it turned its attention south and vanquished the Mamluk Sultanate.
name this empire that seized Mecca under Selim I.

clues.clue: A weeklong battle against the Safavids fought continuously by torchlight was called the Battle of the Torches
examples: A battle between these two powers continued both day and night for a week, giving it the appropriate name Battle of the Torches.

clues.clue: The two empires signed the Treaty of Zuhab, a consolidation of the earlier Peace of Amasya
examples: These two powers signed the Treaty of Zuhab with each other, which was a consolidation of the earlier Peace of Amasya signed between them.
They signed the Peace of Amasya after fighting the Battle of Chaldiran.

clues.clue: The Frenchman Gabriel de Luetz advised one side during a siege of the Safavid city of Van
examples: The Frenchman Gabriel de Luetz helped advise one of these powers during its siege of the other's city of Van.

clues.clue: The empire persecuted remaining Qizilbash and Alevis after Chaldiran, driving Alevis underground
examples: This non-Persian empire persecuted the remaining qizilbash in lands that it gained following its decisive victory at the Battle of Chaldiran.
Mass murder committed by this empire drove the Alevis underground.

works.name: Government and Imperial Institutions
works.description: The empire was administered through a distinctive set of institutions. The devshirme system took Christian boys, especially from the Balkans, and converted them into administrators or Janissary soldiers via Enderun palace schools; the ablest could rise to Grand Vizier. Conquered land was organized into sanjaks (grouped into eyalets, later vilayets) and distributed as timar fiefs to sipahi cavalrymen in exchange for military service. The sultan's court centered on Topkapi Palace, whose secluded imperial harem was overseen by the Valide Sultan (queen mother) and guarded by the Chief Black Eunuch (Kizlar Agha); a period when royal mothers like Roxelana, Nurbanu, and Turhan wielded outsized power became known as the "Sultanate of Women." Legal authority rested on regional kanunname codes and, later, the Mecelle code.

clues.clue: The devshirme system took Christian boys (often from the Balkans) and converted them, via Enderun palace schools, into administrators or Janissary soldiers; the highest position a devshirme recruit could reach was Grand Vizier
examples: name this empire whose devshirme system turned Christian minority children into Janissaries.
This was the highest position a Devshirme could achieve. This most important minister was dismissible only by the Sultan and held the imperial seal.

clues.clue: Conquered land was divided into semi-independent sanjaks (grouped into eyalets, later reformed into the vilayet system) and distributed as timar fiefs to sipahi cavalrymen in exchange for military service
examples: This polity divided newly conquered territory into semi-independent sanjaks.
This polity exchanged fiefs called timar for military service with sipahi cavalrymen.

clues.clue: The imperial harem was secluded within Topkapi Palace; the Valide Sultan (ruler's mother) held the highest female rank, guarded by the Chief Black Eunuch (Kizlar Agha), with Kadin as another harem rank
examples: This empire's secluded imperial harem of concubines lived secluded in the Topkapı Palace.
During the later period of this empire, the Kadin were part of a group guarded by traditionally black eunuchs called Kizlar agha.

clues.clue: A period called the "Sultanate of Women," beginning with Suleiman's marriage to Roxelana, saw royal mothers like Nurbanu and Turhan hold power as Valide
examples: A period of this empire's history called the 'Sultanate of Women' began with its ruler's marriage to Roxelana.
Mothers of the rulers of this empire like Nurbanu and Turhan held power as Valide.

clues.clue: Legal authority rested on regional kanunname codes and, in the empire's later period, the Mecelle code
examples: This empire used a legal system in which each region had a kanunname, and, in the later parts of its existence, it operated under the Mecelle code.

clues.clue: The sultan's head calligrapher (a nisanci) created an ornate monogram called the tughra
examples: These people's head calligrapher was a nisancı and created a monogram called the tughra.

works.name: Religious Minorities: Jewish and Christian Communities
works.description: The millet system granted religious minorities (Christians and Jews) autonomy over their own internal affairs under Islamic rule. The empire took in the largest proportion of Jews expelled by the Alhambra Decree, settling many in cities like Sarajevo, Salonika, and Istanbul, where the Jewish quarter of Balat flourished and Jewish immigration spurred the development of the Ladino language. Individual Jewish figures gained prominence, including the diplomat Joseph Nasi and a merchant nicknamed "Son of the Devil" for monopolizing the salt and fur trade, while other episodes -- such as the Damascus affair blood libel and persecution that drove David Sassoon to the British Empire -- show the limits of that tolerance.

clues.clue: The empire took in the largest proportion of Jews expelled under the Alhambra Decree, settling many in cities like Sarajevo, Salonika, and Istanbul; Jewish immigration prompted development of the Ladino language
examples: name this empire, which took in the largest proportion of Jews after the Alhambra Decree in cities like Sarajevo, Salonika, and Istanbul.
Jewish immigration to this empire at the end of the 15th century prompted the development of the Ladino language.

clues.clue: The millet system governed minorities (Christian and Jewish) with internal autonomy, later curtailed by the Tanzimat reforms
examples: Minority Jewish and Christian communities were tolerated under this empire's millet system.
As with the various denominations of Christians in the empire, this system granted the Ottoman Jewish community much autonomy over their internal affairs.

clues.clue: Jewish merchants flourished in the Balat district of the capital; a Greek-speaking merchant gained the epithet "Son of the Devil" by monopolizing the salt and fur trade there
examples: Jewish merchants flourished in the Balat district of this empire's capital.
A Greek-speaking merchant in this polity gained the epithet 'Son of the Devil' after monopolizing the salt and fur trade while based in a wealthy district of its capital.

clues.clue: The Portuguese Jew Joseph Nasi became a powerful diplomat and reformer at the Ottoman court
examples: The Portuguese Jew Joseph Nasi became a powerful diplomat and reformer while working in the court of this polity.

clues.clue: French consul Ulysse de Ratti-Menton supported a blood libel accusation (the Damascus affair) after the disappearance of Father Thomas
examples: French consul Ulysse de Ratti-Menton supported an accusation of blood libel in this state after the disappearance of the Italian Father Thomas.

clues.clue: Persecution by a regional governor prompted the merchant David Sassoon to move to the British Empire, where his family's opium-trade fortune later funded a mansion he named Sans Souci
examples: Persecution by a regional governor of this empire prompted David Sassoon to move to the British Empire.
A Jewish merchant born in this state emigrated to Mumbai and became active in the opium trade, which in part funded his purchase of a mansion he named Sans Souci.

works.name: Decline, the "Sick Man of Europe," and Historiography
works.description: From roughly the late 16th century, the empire entered a long period Western historians labeled "decline." Bernard Lewis cited the Kafes system (which confined potential heirs in seclusion instead of executing rivals) and a shift from the timariot system to tax-farming as symptoms, while contemporary Janissaries blamed their own diminished authority during the "Sultanate of Women." By the 19th century the empire was widely nicknamed the "sick man of Europe"; William Gladstone's Midlothian campaign attacked Benjamin Disraeli's support for it after the Bulgarian Horrors, and Punch magazine mocked it as a shocked "limited company."

clues.clue: Bernard Lewis cited the Kafes system (secluding, rather than executing, potential heirs) and the shift from the timariot system to tax-farming as evidence of the empire's "decline" from its 16th-century peak
examples: Keeping imperial successors in the Kafes and the transition from the timariot system to tax-farming were cited as elements of this empire's 'decline' from its 16th-century peak by Bernard Lewis.
Give this title, whose holders were raised in seclusion as part of the kafes system.

clues.clue: The empire's "decline thesis" was traced by contemporary Janissaries to their own diminished authority during the "Sultanate of Women"
examples: name this empire, whose 'decline thesis' was started by Janissaries complaining about their decreased authority during its 'sultanate of women.'

clues.clue: By the 19th century the empire was nicknamed the "sick man of Europe"; William Gladstone's Midlothian campaign attacked Benjamin Disraeli's support for it after the Bulgarian Horrors, and Punch magazine depicted it shocked at becoming a "limited company"
examples: Known as the 'sick man of Europe,' it massacred numerous Bulgarians and Armenians under Sultan Abdul Hamid II.
Its leader was once portrayed in Punch magazine looking shocked that this empire had become a 'limited company.'

clues.clue: The bureaucratic apparatus was beset by tax-farming, and it kidnapped Christian boys for the devshirme system while besieging Vienna twice
examples: The bureaucratic apparatus of this other empire was beset by 'tax farming.' Within Europe, it kidnapped Christian boys who became janissaries and besieged Vienna twice.

works.name: Russo-Ottoman Wars and Territorial Losses
works.description: The empire fought a long series of wars against the Russian Empire from the 18th through 19th centuries. Peter the Great's army was defeated in the 1711 Pruth River Campaign, but later wars went the other way: the 1768-74 war ended with the Treaty of Kucuk Kainarji, ceding the Crimean Khanate's independence (soon annexed by Russia) and naming Russia protector of the empire's Orthodox Christians, while the Treaty of Edirne later confirmed the cession of Azov. The final Russo-Turkish War (1877-78) ended with the Treaty of San Stefano, freeing Serbia, Montenegro, and Romania, subsequently revised by the Treaty of Berlin.

clues.clue: Peter the Great's army was defeated by Ottoman forces (under Grand Vizier Baltadji Mehmet Pasha) in the 1711 Pruth River Campaign; Charles XII of Sweden was later exiled to the Ottoman fortress of Bender after his own defeat at Perevolochna
examples: Peter the Great's army was defeated by this polity's forces in the Pruth River Campaign.
After surrendering at Perevolochna, Charles XII of Sweden was exiled to this polity's fortress of Bender.

clues.clue: The 1768-74 Russo-Turkish war ended with the Treaty of Kucuk Kainarji, making the Crimean Khanate a nominally independent Russian satellite (later annexed) and naming Russia protector of Orthodox Christians in the empire; the Treaty of Edirne later confirmed cession of the Azov region
examples: It gave Kerch, the northern Caucasus, and Southern Ukraine to Russia and made the Crimean Khanate a Russian satellite. It also made Russia the protector of Orthodox Christians in the Ottoman Empire.
The region of Azov was ceded to this polity in a treaty stipulating the destruction of strategic castles like Taganrog that was confirmed by the Treaty of Edirne.

clues.clue: The final Russo-Turkish War (1877-78) ended with the Treaty of San Stefano, which freed Serbia, Montenegro, and Romania from Ottoman control; it was revised four months later by the Treaty of Berlin
examples: The Treaty of San Stefano was signed after one of these powers nearly invaded the other's capital.
This was modified four months later by the Treaty of Berlin.

clues.clue: Earlier wars against Russia were marked by treaties at Jassy and by the 1774 Treaty of Kucuk-Kainarji, plus the 1833 Treaty of Unkiar Skelessi, which secretly let the empire close the Dardanelles at Russia's discretion
examples: Important turning points in the wars between these two powers came with the treaties of Jassy and Kuchuk-Kainarji.
Name this empire that previously signed the Treaty of Unkiar Skelessi with the Russians, which included a secret article allowing it to close the Dardanelles at Russia's discretion.

clues.clue: In a naval battle, Russian admiral Samuel Greig surprised Ottoman forces by sailing fire-ships into their fleet at night
examples: In a battle between these two powers, Samuel Greig surprised the opposing side by sailing his fire-ships into their fleet in the dead of night.

works.name: Tanzimat Reforms and 19th-Century Nationalist Uprisings
works.description: The 19th century brought both top-down modernization and bottom-up nationalist revolt. The Tanzimat reform era, begun by the Rescript of the Rose Chamber and pursued by Grand Vizier Mustafa Resid Pasha, abolished tax-farming and the jizya tax, restructured sanjaks into equal-population units, and curtailed the millet system to blunt nationalism; it followed Sultan Mahmud II's 1826 Auspicious Incident, which violently ended the Janissary corps. Meanwhile the empire lost the Greek War of Independence (climaxing at the Battle of Navarino), suffered international outrage over the suppression of Bulgaria's April Uprising (the "Bulgarian Horrors"), and, under Sultan Abdul Hamid II, was accused of massacring Bulgarians and Armenians as Albanian nationalists in the League of Prizren likewise resisted Ottoman rule.

clues.clue: The Tanzimat reforms began with the Rescript of the Rose Chamber (promising an end to tax-farming) and abolished the jizya tax, gave equal status to Phanariotes and Armenians, and curbed the millet system to reduce nationalism; they were masterminded by Grand Vizier Mustafa Resid Pasha, who negotiated the Treaty of Balta Limani with Britain
examples: The Rescript of the Rose Chamber promised the end of tax farming during this period, during which changes in the millet system attempted to curb nationalism among religious minorities.
The Tanzimat reforms were masterminded by this leader who served as Grand Vizier six times and negotiated the Treaty of Balta Liman with Britain.

clues.clue: The 1826 Auspicious Incident violently ended the Janissary corps under Sultan Mahmud II, who had earlier tried to create a New Order army; vilayets (replacing eyalets) and other modernizing measures followed
examples: During the 19th century, this empire underwent the Tanzimat reform era after the Auspicious Incident ended the prevalence of its Janissary infantry corps.
Before the Auspicious Incident, the Janissaries proved increasingly resistant to reform, such as this Sultan's attempt to create a New Order army.

clues.clue: The empire lost the Greek War of Independence, achieved after a combined British, French, and Russian fleet defeated the Ottoman-Egyptian fleet (led by Ibrahim Pasha) at the 1827 Battle of Navarino
examples: Bandits called klephts fought for this cause, which was achieved after a combined British, French, and Russian fleet won the Battle of Navarino.
Edward Codrington scored a decisive victory over the Ottoman fleet, which was led by Ibrahim Pasha, at this 1827 naval battle.

clues.clue: Suppression of Bulgaria's April Uprising by bashi-bazouk irregular troops caused international outrage (the "Bulgarian Horrors"), which William Gladstone used to attack Benjamin Disraeli's pro-Ottoman policy
examples: During this nation's April Uprising, bashi-bazouk troops massacred citizens, leading William Gladstone to criticize Benjamin Disraeli for failing to address this country's 'horrors.'
Rebels from this nation built cannons made of cherrywood during an uprising whose brutal suppression was reported on by Eugene Schuyler.

clues.clue: Under Sultan Abdul Hamid II, the empire was accused of massacring numerous Bulgarians and Armenians
examples: Known as the 'sick man of Europe,' it massacred numerous Bulgarians and Armenians under Sultan Abdul Hamid II.

clues.clue: The Albanian League of Prizren advocated Muslim solidarity but resisted Ottoman rule when it ceded Albanian lands to Montenegro
examples: Though the League of Prizren advocated Muslim solidarity, they fought these overlords to stop their cession of Albanian lands to Montenegro.

works.name: World War I, Partition, and the End of the Empire
works.description: The 1908 Young Turk Revolution preceded the empire's final collapse. During World War I, it built the Hejaz Railway linking Damascus to Medina to support Levantine pilgrimage and control (later disrupted by T. E. Lawrence's forces during the Arab Revolt), and repelled a costly Allied invasion at Gallipoli. Defeat brought the Treaty of Sevres and secret Sykes-Picot arrangement, which carved up the empire's Middle Eastern territory (formalized further at the San Remo Conference over oil profits) despite promises made in the McMahon-Hussein correspondence; the Armistice of Mudanya and the Karakol Organization's resistance to partition led instead to the Treaty of Lausanne. Mustafa Kemal Ataturk then abolished the sultanate, negotiated the Republic of Turkey's recognition, and in 1924 abolished the caliphate (a title the Ottoman sultans had held since Selim I), events chronicled in his 36-hour Nutuk speech.

clues.clue: The 1908 Young Turk Revolution occurred in the empire
examples: the Young Turk Revolution occurred in what empire?

clues.clue: The empire built the Hejaz Railway linking Damascus to Medina to support pilgrimage and control of its Levantine/Arabian possessions; T. E. Lawrence's forces used explosives to disrupt it during the Arab Revolt
examples: This empire connected its Levantine possessions to pilgrimage sites by constructing the Hejaz railway.
During the Arab Revolt, T. E. Lawrence's forces made use of explosives devised by this British officer, whose namesake mines were used to disrupt the Hejaz Railway.

clues.clue: British forces attacked the Gallipoli peninsula (north of the Dardanelles) in a costly World War I campaign, birthplace of the ANZAC "legend"
examples: name this peninsula north of the Dardanelles that British forces attacked in a costly World War I campaign.
This place is regarded as the birthplace of a nationalistic 'legend' named for the joint army corps ANZAC.

clues.clue: Postwar partition of the empire was carried out via the Treaty of Sevres and the secret Sykes-Picot Treaty, splitting oil profits at the San Remo Conference despite promises made in the McMahon-Hussein Correspondence; the process was ended by the Armistice of Mudanya
examples: name this action which was undertaken by the Treaty of Sevres and the Sykes-Picot Treaty and involved carving up a Middle Eastern power following World War I.
At the San Remo Conference, two participants agreed to split oil profits from taking this action.

clues.clue: Mustafa Kemal Ataturk negotiated the empire's dissolution at the 1923 Conference/Treaty of Lausanne (replacing Sevres), affirming his "Six Arrows" ideology; the empire's final days were described in his 36-hour Nutuk speech
examples: This second Turkish president negotiated the dissolution of the Ottoman Empire at Lausanne and was responsible for affirming the 'Six Arrows' ideology.
An account of the final days of this empire was presented in the 36-hour Nutuk speech.

clues.clue: The Ottoman sultan had held the title of caliph for 500 years since Selim I; it was abolished by a 1924 law shortly after Sharif Hussein bin Ali briefly claimed the title
examples: Sharif Hussein bin Ali briefly took this title, which was held by the Ottoman Sultan for 500 years from Selim I until its abolition by Ataturk.
name this title alternately called 'Commander of the Faithful.'

works.name: Legacy: Literary and Artistic Depictions
works.description: The empire's cultural legacy is prominent in later literature and art. Orhan Pamuk's novel My Name is Red is set among miniaturist painters during the reign of Sultan Murad III and centers on the murder of the miniaturist Elegant Effendi, narrated in part by the lovers Black and Shekure and by the fabric-seller Esther; his novel The White Castle also uses an Ottoman setting. Ivo Andric's The Bridge on the Drina follows the Ottoman-built bridge (kapia) at Visegrad across centuries, including the "blood tribute" of the devshirme system and the overseer Abidaga's forced-labor construction of the bridge. Other depictions include Ismail Kadare's The Palace of Dreams, Mesa Selimovic's Death and the Dervish, Ahmet Hamdi Tanpinar's The Time Regulation Institute, and Ilya Repin's painting The Reply of the Zaporozhian Cossacks.

clues.clue: Orhan Pamuk's novel My Name is Red is set among miniaturist painters at the Ottoman court during the reign of Sultan Murad III, centering on the murder of the miniaturist Elegant Effendi (suspects include Olive, Butterfly, and Stork) and narrated in part by the coin, Satan, and the color red itself
examples: name this empire where Orhan Pamuk set My Name is Red, which is set during the reign of Sultan Murad III.
The narrators of a novel set in this empire include the murdered miniaturist Elegant Effendi, a coin, Satan, and the title color.

clues.clue: In My Name is Red, the fabric-seller Esther carries love letters between the lovers Shekure and Black, whose romance is patterned on Nizami Ganjavi's Khosrau and Shirin
examples: In a novel set during the reign of this empire, the fabric seller Esther helps to arrange marriages by carrying the letters of lovers back and forth.
The love story between Shekure and Black in a novel set in this empire is patterned deliberately on Nizami Ganjavi's Khosrau and Shirin.

clues.clue: Ivo Andric's The Bridge on the Drina centers on a bridge (with a central platform called a kapia) built in the empire's Balkan territory, whose forced-labor construction under the overseer Abidaga is called the empire's "blood tribute"
examples: an empire which built the title structure of Ivo Andric's The Bridge on the Drina using forced Christian labor.
A victim of this empire's 'blood tribute' orders Abidaga to conscript peasants to cart the stones for a kapia that stands for 350 years.

clues.clue: In Orhan Pamuk's The White Castle, the scientist Hoja unsuccessfully besieges a fortress with a war machine before escaping the empire's watch
examples: After unsuccessfully besieging the title structure with his war machine, the scientist Hoja escapes the eye of this empire, in the novel The White Castle.

clues.clue: Ismail Kadare's The Palace of Dreams (set anachronistically in the empire) follows Mark-Alem, who works at a bureaucracy studying people's dreams
examples: Mark-Alem works at a government bureaucracy studying people's dreams in a thinly-veiled portrait of this author's home country in his novel The Palace of Dreams.
The Palace of Dreams is set during this empire, though it has many historical anachronisms.

clues.clue: Mesa Selimovic's novel Death and the Dervish follows a title character who leaves his monastery to avenge his brother's execution without trial by Ottoman authorities
examples: The title character leaves his monastery to avenge the execution without trial of his brother by authorities from this empire in a novel by Mesa Selimovic.

clues.clue: Ahmet Hamdi Tanpinar's novel The Time Regulation Institute follows Hayri Irdal, employed to set clocks to Western time as part of the title bizarre bureaucracy
examples: Name this Ahmet Hamdi Tanpınar novel about Hayri Irdal, who is employed to set clocks to Western time as part of the title bizarre bureaucracy.

clues.clue: Ilya Repin's painting The Reply of the Zaporozhian Cossacks depicts Cossacks laughing while drafting an insult-laden letter of defiance to Sultan Mehmed IV; it was the most expensive painting ever sold in Russia after Alexander III bought it
examples: In this painting, a fat man in a red suit and a white fur hat laughs as the title group dictates a list of insults such as 'Goat-fucker of Alexandria' and 'catamite of Tartary.'
This painting was the most expensive painting ever sold in its country after Alexander III bought it for thirty-five thousand rubles.

clues.clue: Voltaire's Candide has Pangloss question a dervish about human suffering while traveling with Paquette and Cunegonde in the empire; Alexandre Dumas's The Count of Monte Cristo has the pasha Ali Pasha attempt to break from the empire, resulting in his daughter Haydee being sold into slavery
examples: Pangloss asks a dervish about the reason for man's suffering when he travels to the Ottoman Empire with Paquette, Cunegonde, and the title character of this Voltaire novel.
Name this empire, from which Ali Pasha attempts to break away, but fails and gets his daughter Haydee sold into slavery in The Count of Monte Cristo.

comprehensive_summary: The Ottoman Empire was founded around 1299 by Osman I, chief of the Kayı tribe of Oghuz Turks, in the region of Bithynia, out of the wreck of the Mongol-shattered Sultanate of Rum. According to legend, Osman dreamed of a moon entering his chest and a tree growing from his navel to shade the world, foretelling the empire's rise; his first military victory came at the Battle of Bapheus. Historians dispute the motor of this early growth: Paul Wittek's discredited Ghaza thesis credited holy war, while Rudi Paul Lindner emphasized tribal organization and assimilation, and Colin Imber called the period a documentary black hole. The empire's decisive early triumph came in 1453, when Mehmed II captured Constantinople with the aid of the giant Basilic cannon (acquired through negotiations with the Hungarian engineer Orban), ending the Byzantine Empire and moving the Ottoman capital there from Edirne; he later appointed the Christian Patriarch Gennadius II to power within the empire. The empire reached its zenith under Suleiman the Magnificent ("the Lawgiver"), who controversially allied with Francis I of France against their shared Habsburg rivals, fought at Mohacs, and saw his admiral Hayreddin Barbarossa crush Andrea Doria at the 1538 Battle of Preveza, securing Mediterranean naval supremacy until the 1571 Battle of Lepanto (a 1565 siege of Malta was repelled by the Knights Hospitaller). Beyond the Mediterranean, the cartographer-admiral Piri Reis fought Portugal for the Indian Ocean, recapturing Aden but failing at Hormuz, and compiled the Kitab-i Bahriye and a celebrated 1513 world map. As a Sunni power, the empire repeatedly clashed with the Shia Safavid Empire, most decisively when Selim I ("the Grim") crushed Shah Ismail I's Qizilbash at the 1514 Battle of Chaldiran before conquering the Mamluk Sultanate and taking the title of caliph; further fighting included the week-long Battle of the Torches and treaties at Amasya and Zuhab. Domestically, the empire ran on distinctive institutions: the devshirme system converted Christian boys (often Balkan) into Janissary soldiers or administrators who could rise as high as Grand Vizier; conquered land was divided into sanjaks and distributed as timar fiefs to sipahi cavalry; and the sultan's court centered on Topkapi Palace, where the Valide Sultan led the secluded imperial harem during a period known as the Sultanate of Women (beginning with Suleiman's marriage to Roxelana). The millet system gave religious minorities internal autonomy, and the empire took in the largest share of Jews expelled by the Alhambra Decree, developing communities that spoke Ladino and produced figures like the diplomat Joseph Nasi, though episodes like the Damascus affair blood libel showed the limits of tolerance. From the late 16th century the empire entered a long historiographical "decline," which Bernard Lewis attributed to the Kafes seclusion system and a shift to tax-farming; by the 19th century it was nicknamed the "sick man of Europe," a label at the center of British debates like Gladstone's Midlothian campaign. The empire fought a long series of wars against Russia, losing the Crimean Khanate's independence at the Treaty of Kucuk Kainarji (after Peter the Great had earlier been defeated in the 1711 Pruth River Campaign) and finally losing Serbia, Montenegro, and Romania at the Treaty of San Stefano. The 19th century also brought the Tanzimat reforms -- begun by the Rescript of the Rose Chamber and pursued by Grand Vizier Mustafa Resid Pasha after Sultan Mahmud II's 1826 Auspicious Incident destroyed the Janissary corps -- even as nationalist revolts stripped away Balkan territory, from the Greek War of Independence (won at the Battle of Navarino) to Bulgaria's April Uprising and Albania's League of Prizren. The empire's final act began with the 1908 Young Turk Revolution and continued through World War I, when it built the Hejaz Railway (disrupted by T. E. Lawrence during the Arab Revolt) and repelled the Allied Gallipoli campaign, only to be dismantled after defeat via the Treaty of Sevres and secret Sykes-Picot arrangement. Mustafa Kemal Ataturk negotiated a final settlement at the Treaty of Lausanne, abolished the sultanate, and in 1924 ended the caliphate the Ottoman sultans had held since Selim I. The empire's legacy pervades later literature and art, from Orhan Pamuk's My Name is Red (set among Ottoman court miniaturists under Murad III) and The White Castle, to Ivo Andric's The Bridge on the Drina, Ismail Kadare's The Palace of Dreams, Mesa Selimovic's Death and the Dervish, Ahmet Hamdi Tanpinar's The Time Regulation Institute, and Ilya Repin's painting The Reply of the Zaporozhian Cossacks.
recursive_suggestions: Orhan Pamuk -- author of My Name is Red and The White Castle, both set in the empire; distinct Literature topic
Ivo Andric -- author of The Bridge on the Drina; distinct Literature topic
Suleiman the Magnificent -- the empire's most-clued ruler; could warrant a standalone History figure page
Mehmed II -- conqueror of Constantinople; standalone figure candidate
Battle of Chaldiran / Safavid Empire -- recurring rival power with its own dense cluster
Greek War of Independence -- separate answerline with its own dense cluster of clues
Tanzimat (period/reforms) -- appeared as its own distinct answerline
Gallipoli campaign -- appeared as its own distinct answerline (Gallipoli peninsula)
Mustafa Kemal Ataturk -- founder of the Republic of Turkey; distinct History figure topic
Devshirme/Janissaries -- recurring institutional cluster; could support a dedicated card set if it recurs across other empire/period topics
Piri Reis -- cartographer/admiral with a dense standalone clue cluster

Ambiguity flag: 'Turkey' and 'Republic of Turkey' appeared as separate modern-country answerlines (clued mostly via Janissary-band music and Iznik ware/art) and were excluded from this page -- filter to History + Ottoman Empire specifically when searching.
